perturbation.original_text: Advertising/Marketing/Educational Expenses will include (a) King's out-of-pocket costs for Samples incurred as contemplated by Section 6.5, (b) all out-of-pocket costs for Promotional Materials and training materials, and (c) out-of-pocket costs for the purchase of the Prescriber Data. Advertising/Marketing/Educational Expenses will not include (i) any expenses of the King Sales Force or Depomed Sales Force, (ii) any costs incurred by Depomed with respect to the Depomed Sales Force, including as described in Section 4.9(g), or (iii) any costs for the personnel of King or Depomed.
perturbation.changed_text: Advertising/Marketing/Educational Expenses will include (a) King's out-of-pocket costs for Samples incurred as contemplated by Section 6.5, (b) all costs for Promotional Materials and training materials, and (c) all costs for the purchase of the Prescriber Data. Advertising/Marketing/Educational Expenses will not include (i) any expenses of the King Sales Force or Depomed Sales Force, (ii) any costs incurred by Depomed with respect to the Depomed Sales Force, including as described in Section 4.9(g), or (iii) any costs for the personnel of King or Depomed.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'out-of-pocket' from the expenses included, this introduces a legal contradiction. Certain laws specify that only direct, out-of-pocket expenses can be considered bona fide marketing expenses for tax or regulatory purposes. Now the company can include fixed costs, personnel costs, and overhead costs as marketing expenses. This creates uncertainty and potential non-compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 26 U.S. Code § 162 - Trade or business expenses
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text potentially allows for the inclusion of expenses beyond the 'ordinary and necessary' business expenses directly attributable to advertising, marketing, and education, which is contrary to the IRS's interpretation and enforcement of Section 162. It could lead to improper deductions for expenses that are not directly related or are capital expenditures. It also implies that the costs can now include things that aren't strictly considered part of Advertising/Marketing/Educational expenses.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 162 - Trade or business expenses | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 162 - Trade or business expenses U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) In general There shall be allowed as a deduction all the ordinary and necessary expenses paid or incurred during the taxable year in carrying on any trade or business, including— (1) a reasonable allowance for salaries or other compensation for personal services actually rendered; (2) traveling expenses (including amounts expended for meals and lodging other than amounts which are lavish or extravagant under the circumstances) while away from home in the pursuit of a trade or business; and (3) rentals or other payments required to be made as a condition to the continued use or possession, for purposes of the trade or business, of property to which the taxpayer has not taken or is not taking title or in which he has no equity. For purposes of the preceding sentence, the place of residence of a Member of Congress (including any Delegate and Resident Commissioner) within the State, congressional district, or possession which he represents in Congress shall be considered his home, but amounts expended by such Members within each taxable year for living expenses shall not be deductible for income tax purposes. For purposes of paragraph (2), the taxpayer shall not be treated as being temporarily away from home during any period of employment if such period exceeds 1 year. The preceding sentence shall not apply to any Federal employee during any period for which such employee is certified by the Attorney General (or the designee thereof) as traveling on behalf of the United States in temporary duty status to investigate or prosecute, or provide support services for the investigation or prosecution of, a Federal crime. (b) Charitable contributions and gifts excepted No deduction shall be allowed under
perturbation.justification: The modified text, by removing 'out-of-pocket' from the included expenses, contradicts IRS guidelines on deductible business expenses. The Law Explanation states that the modified text allows expenses beyond 'ordinary and necessary' business expenses, contradicting the IRS's interpretation of Section 162. Snippet 1 and Snippet 2 both reference 26 USC 162, stating, "There shall be allowed as a deduction all the ordinary and necessary expenses paid or incurred during the taxable year in carrying on any trade or business..." Removing 'out-of-pocket' allows for a broader range of expenses, potentially including those not 'ordinary and necessary,' thus creating a contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this change could allow fixed costs, personnel costs, and overhead costs to be included as marketing expenses, leading to non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: Section 1.91 "Serious Adverse Drug Experience" means any Adverse Drug Experience, including those subject to expedited reporting as defined in the regulations cited below, that is fatal or life-threatening, requires hospitalization or prolongation of existing hospitalization, results in persistent or significant disability or incapacity, is a congenital anomaly/birth defect, or is of comparable medical significance or any other event which would constitute a "serious" Adverse Drug Experience pursuant to the terms of 21 C.F.R. 314.80 or 312.32.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 1.91 "Serious Adverse Drug Experience" means any Adverse Drug Experience, including those subject to routine reporting, that is fatal or life-threatening, requires hospitalization or prolongation of existing hospitalization, results in persistent or significant disability or incapacity, is a congenital anomaly/birth defect, or is of comparable medical significance or any other event.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces 'expedited reporting' with 'routine reporting' and removes reference to specific CFR sections. This contradicts federal regulations that mandate specific expedited reporting timelines for serious adverse events, creating uncertainty about compliance. It also removes the specific standards set by the code, in effect negating the specific guidelines set by law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR § 314.80 - Postmarketing reporting of adverse drug experiences
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 314.80 outlines specific requirements and timelines for expedited reporting of serious and unexpected adverse drug experiences. The modified text's shift to 'routine reporting' directly contradicts these requirements, potentially leading to delays in reporting critical safety information to the FDA and non-compliance with federal law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'expedited reporting' with 'routine reporting,' contradicting 21 CFR § 314.80, which outlines specific requirements and timelines for expedited reporting of serious and unexpected adverse drug experiences. By changing 'expedited' to 'routine,' the modification undermines the legally mandated rapid reporting of serious adverse events, potentially causing non-compliance with federal regulations and delaying critical safety information to the FDA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 21 CFR 314.80, the regulation at the heart of this contradiction. The change directly opposes the purpose of expedited reporting as required by federal law.

perturbation.original_text: Section 5.3 Compliance. In performing its duties hereunder, each party shall, and shall cause the King Sales Force or Depomed Sales Force, as applicable, and its employees and agents to, comply with all Legal Requirements, including the FDA's regulations and guidelines concerning the advertising of prescription drug products, DDMAC's promotional guidelines, the Department of Health and Human Services Office of the Inspector General Compliance Program Guidance for Pharmaceutical Manufacturers, the American Medical Association's Guidelines on Gifts to Physicians, the PhRMA Code on Interactions with Healthcare Providers, the Prescription Drug Marketing Act of 1987, as amended, and the rules and regulations promulgated thereunder, the ACCME Standards for Commercial Support of Continuing Medical Education, equal employment, non-discrimination and federal and state anti-kickback Legal Requirements, Legal Requirements with respect to submission of false claims to governmental or private health care payors, and all industry and professional standards, which may be applicable to the activities (including the warehousing, handling and distribution of Samples) to be performed by such party hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 5.3 Compliance. In performing its duties hereunder, each party shall, and shall cause the King Sales Force or Depomed Sales Force, as applicable, and its employees and agents to, comply with all best efforts towards Legal Requirements, including some of the FDA's regulations and guidelines concerning the advertising of prescription drug products, some of DDMAC’s promotional guidelines, the Department of Health and Human Services Office of the Inspector General Compliance Program Guidance for Pharmaceutical Manufacturers, the American Medical Association's Guidelines on Gifts to Physicians, the PhRMA Code on Interactions with Healthcare Providers, the Prescription Drug Marketing Act of 1987, as amended, and the rules and regulations promulgated thereunder, the ACCME Standards for Commercial Support of Continuing Medical Education, equal employment, non-discrimination and federal and state anti-kickback Legal Requirements, Legal Requirements with respect to submission of false claims to governmental or private health care payors, and all industry and professional standards, which may be applicable to the activities (including the warehousing, handling and distribution of Samples) to be performed by such party hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'comply with all Legal Requirements' to 'comply with all best efforts towards Legal Requirements, including some of the FDA's regulations' introduces ambiguity and weakens the compliance obligation. This creates a contradiction because regulations like those under the Prescription Drug Marketing Act (PDMA) require strict compliance, not just 'best efforts'. This introduces uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: The PDMA sets forth specific controls and requirements regarding the distribution of prescription drug samples. Changing 'comply with all' to 'best efforts towards' directly undermines the mandatory nature of these controls. It implies the parties can selectively comply and may not be held fully accountable for adhering to sample distribution restrictions, record-keeping, and other critical obligations outlined in the PDMA.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'comply with all Legal Requirements' to 'comply with all best efforts towards Legal Requirements' introduces a significant weakening of the compliance obligation, creating a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation highlights that the PDMA requires specific controls and mandatory obligations regarding the distribution of prescription drug samples. The modified text implies that parties can selectively comply and may not be fully accountable for adhering to sample distribution restrictions. The scraped snippet does not offer enough insight for further support.
